source.title: Development and characterization of a glycine biosensor system for fine-tuned metabolic regulation in Escherichia coli

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study addresses the lack of in vivo biosensors for glycine in E. coli, limiting dynamic metabolic regulation and directed evolution. The goal is to develop synthetic glycine-ON and -OFF riboswitches with improved dynamic range, sensitivity, and orthogonality for metabolic engineering applications.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the development of synthetic glycine-ON (glyON14) and -OFF (glyOFF6) riboswitches from a B. subtilis glycine riboswitch library using FACS and tetA-based dual genetic selection. The riboswitches were applied in tunable lactate regulation, dynamic serine synthesis regulation, and directed evolution of alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase (AGXT), yielding mutants with 58% and 73% increased activity, which improved 5-aminolevulinic acid yield.
process_signals: Problem: insufficient biosensors for metabolites
Solution: engineered glycine riboswitches
Key results: glyON14 with increased detection range (0-107 mM), glyOFF6 with >10-fold repression
Applications validated: tunable regulation, dynamic regulation, directed evolution

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Highlights the need for dynamic regulation in metabolic engineering to avoid metabolic burden and growth arrest from static control. Biosensors, especially riboswitches, are promising due to short response times and low burden. Natural glycine riboswitches exist but have limited engineering for metabolic pathways. The study aims to engineer glycine riboswitches for E. coli to expand the biosensor toolkit.
process_signals: Problem: static control causes metabolic burden; few biosensors for many metabolites
Existing solutions: combinatorial libraries (static), TF-based, FRET-based biosensors
Riboswitch advantages: short response time, low burden, strict regulation
Gap: glycine riboswitches not widely used for metabolic engineering
Hypothesis: natural glycine riboswitches can be engineered for improved performance
failures_or_limits: Glycine riboswitch applications mostly for protein production, not metabolic pathway control

section_gists.heading: Selection and construction of an in vivo glycine riboswitch suitable for E. coli
section_gists.gist: Screened four natural glycine riboswitches (B. subtilis, S. pyogenes, V. cholerae, F. nucleatum) in E. coli with GFP reporter. Only B. subtilis riboswitch functioned as an activator. Synthetic M1-37 promoter gave better dynamic range than native promoter. Constructed tetA-based dual genetic selection cassette for library screening, validated with Ni2+ sensitivity and tetracycline resistance.
process_signals: Initial screening: only B. subtilis riboswitch worked in E. coli
Other riboswitches failed, possibly due to genetic background differences
Promoter choice: synthetic M1-37 better than native
Dual selection system built for high-throughput screening
methods: Dose-response curves with varying glycine (0-107 mM)
tetA dual selection (tetracycline resistance, NiCl2 sensitivity)
failures_or_limits: Three riboswitches non-functional in E. coli
Glycine toxicity above 107 mM limits concentration range

section_gists.heading: Screening and characterization of glycine-ON and -OFF libraries
section_gists.gist: Generated riboswitch libraries by error-prone PCR. Screened using ON-SCREEN and OFF-SCREEN protocols with FACS and dual selection. Isolated glyON14 (increased detection range to 107 mM, 50% higher GFP) and glyOFF6 (>10-fold repression, 54% higher amplitude). Characterized dose-response and Kd values.
process_signals: Library creation: 4 rounds error-prone PCR
Screening: FACS sorted top 0.05-0.1% fluorescent cells
Secondary screening: 32 ON mutants, 118 OFF mutants with improved ratios
Best variants: glyON14 (wider range, lower sensitivity) and glyOFF6 (stronger repression)
metrics: Kd (49 mM for glyON14, 1.53-fold higher than WT)
failures_or_limits: Many mutants did not improve performance

section_gists.heading: Sequence analysis of the evolved glycine riboswitches
section_gists.gist: Sequenced top 6 ON and OFF variants. glyOFF6 mutations: G42C, A62U, U79C, A92G, U120A; glyON14 mutations: A19G, A47C, U73A, C106U. Mutations located in junctions, glycine binding site, helices, P0/K-turn. High-frequency mutations U120A and A108G/U linked to OFF switch. Second aptamer mutations more frequent in OFF variants.
process_signals: Mutations mapped to structural elements
U120A may convert ON to OFF
P0/K-turn mutations affect binding affinity and detection range
Second aptamer mutations enriched in OFF variants, consistent with literature
methods: Structural mapping to known riboswitch domains

section_gists.heading: Tunable regulation of lactate synthesis in E. coli using a glycine-ON riboswitch
section_gists.gist: Used glyON14 to control ldhA expression in lactate-producing E. coli. Tested high, medium, low copy plasmids. High-copy plasmid gave best gradient response but caused growth burden. Low-copy plasmids saturated at low glycine. Demonstrated tunable lactate production with exogenous glycine.
process_signals: Application validation: tunable control of lactate synthesis
Copy number effect: high-copy better dynamic range but metabolic burden
Low-copy plasmids insensitive at higher glycine
Successful gradient response with high-copy plasmid
methods: Plasmid construction with different copy numbers (pUC18, P15C, pZY48)
failures_or_limits: Low-copy plasmids showed saturation, limited tunability
High-copy plasmid caused metabolic burden and reduced growth

section_gists.heading: Dynamic metabolic regulation using the glycine-OFF riboswitch in E. coli
section_gists.gist: Used glyOFF6 to dynamically control glyA expression in serine-producing E. coli with glyA deletion. Restored growth and increased serine production by 104% (660.48 mg/L) and yield (85.40 mg/g glucose) compared to control. Demonstrated dynamic regulation without chemical inducers.
process_signals: Application: dynamic control of essential gene (glyA) to avoid growth arrest
Result: improved serine titer and yield, better growth
No need for chemical inducers
methods: Strain engineering (glyA deletion, serine pathway overexpression)

section_gists.heading: Directed evolution of alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase using a synthetic high-throughput screening platform based on the glycine-OFF riboswitch
section_gists.gist: Coupled AGXT activity to glycine production, which regulates glyOFF6 controlling tetA. High glycine producers survive Ni2+ selection. Enriched beneficial mutants over 3 cycles. Isolated AGXT22 and AGXT26 with 58% and 73% higher activity. Platform effectively enriched high-activity variants.
process_signals: High-throughput screening: growth-based selection linked to enzyme activity
Enrichment of beneficial mutations over cycles
Isolation of improved mutants
Platform removed neutral/negative mutations

section_gists.heading: Characterization of the evolved alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase AGXT26
section_gists.gist: AGXT26 had 14 mutations, including Arg360Cys near PLP site, Asp201Gly, Asp341Gly in polypeptide binding sites, Ile20Thr affecting subunit interaction, and 4 synonymous mutations. Structural modeling suggested increased stability and possibly altered translation efficiency.
process_signals: Mutations mapped to PLP binding, polypeptide binding, subunit interface
Synonymous mutations may affect translation or folding
Combined effects likely improve activity
failures_or_limits: Causal mutations not definitively identified; some may be hitchhikers

section_gists.heading: Discussions
section_gists.gist: Reiterates the need for dynamic regulation. Discusses success of glyOFF6 in dynamic glyA control, avoiding growth arrest. Highlights high-throughput screening platform for AGXT evolution. Analyzes mutations: GBS mutations alter binding, P0/K-turn mutations affect affinity, second aptamer mutations favor OFF state. Notes synonymous mutations may contribute. Acknowledges need to verify causal mutations.
process_signals: Dynamic regulation superior to static knockout
Screening platform effective for enzyme evolution
Mutation analysis provides mechanistic insights
Limitations: some mutations may be hitchhikers, need further verification
failures_or_limits: Causal vs hitchhiker mutations not fully resolved
Need for further verification of mutation effects

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Successfully developed glyOFF6 and glyON14 riboswitches. Demonstrated applications in tunable regulation, dynamic regulation, and directed evolution. Identified key mutations. High-throughput platform yielded improved AGXT for 5-ALA synthesis. Provides example of modifying natural riboswitch for industrial use.
process_signals: Achieved goal of synthetic glycine riboswitches
Validated multiple applications
Future: further dissect functional domains, improve performance

section_gists.heading: Analytical methods
section_gists.gist: Describes quantification methods: 5-ALA by modified Ehrlich's reagent; glucose, lactate, acetate, formate, serine, pyruvate by HPLC-RID; serine also by OPA derivatization with HPLC-UV.
methods: OPA derivatization and HPLC-UV for serine

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Natural glycine riboswitches can be engineered via directed evolution to create ON and OFF variants with improved dynamic range and repression.
The tetA-based dual genetic selection combined with FACS enables efficient screening of riboswitch libraries.
Engineered riboswitches can be applied for tunable and dynamic metabolic regulation, and as high-throughput screening tools for enzyme evolution.
Mutations in specific structural elements (GBS, P0/K-turn, second aptamer) are key to altering riboswitch function.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Which specific mutations are causal for improved riboswitch performance vs. hitchhikers?
Can the riboswitches be further optimized for industrial conditions (e.g., higher glycine tolerance, different genetic backgrounds)?
What is the detailed mechanism of how mutations affect riboswitch conformation and dynamics?
Are there off-target effects or cross-reactivity with other metabolites?

events.summary: The existing number and performance of biosensors is far from sufficient, limiting their potential applications in metabolic engineering.
Synthetic glycine-ON and -OFF riboswitches can be developed for metabolic regulation and directed evolution in E. coli.
Four glycine riboswitches from different sources were screened in E. coli with GFP reporter; only B. subtilis riboswitch functioned as an activator.
A tetA-based dual genetic selection cassette was constructed using the B. subtilis glycine-ON riboswitch for library screening.
numbers_or_metrics: growth rate μ decreased to 0.045 h^-1 with 90 μM Ni2+

events.summary: Glycine-ON and -OFF plasmid libraries were screened using ON-SCREEN and OFF-SCREEN assays with FACS and dual selection.
Glycine concentration above 107 mM negatively affects growth, limiting the detection range.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers initial screening, promoter selection, dual selection system construction, and library screening setup.

events.summary: Glycine riboswitch library constructed via four rounds of error-prone PCR and Gibson assembly.
ON and OFF libraries screened using ON-SCREEN and OFF-SCREEN assays with FACS and dual selection.
Secondary screening of 200 colonies each from ON and OFF libraries in 96-well plates with GFP assay.
glyOFF6 showed >10-fold repression and 54% higher amplitude; glyON14 showed 50% higher GFP and detection range increased to 107 mM.
Sequencing of top ON and OFF variants revealed mutations in structural elements; U120A may convert ON to OFF.
glyON14 used for tunable regulation of ldhA in lactate-producing E. coli; high-copy plasmid gave best gradient response but caused growth burden.
methods_or_tools: plasmid construction with different copy numbers (pUC18, P15C, pZY48)

events.summary: Low-copy plasmids saturated at low glycine, limiting tunability; high-copy caused metabolic burden.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers library screening, characterization of glyON14 and glyOFF6, sequence analysis, and tunable regulation application.

events.summary: Tunable regulation of lactate synthesis using glyON14 riboswitch in high-copy plasmid pUC18.
Lactate production increased with glycine concentration in strain W105-18A.
numbers_or_metrics: 0.4 g/L at 0 mM, 3.05 g/L at 80 mM

events.summary: Low-copy-number plasmid saturated at low glycine, limiting tunability.
Dynamic regulation of glyA using glyOFF6 in serine-producing strain E4G2.
Dynamic glyA control avoided growth arrest and chemical inducers, improving serine productivity.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers tunable lactate regulation and dynamic serine regulation applications of engineered riboswitches.

events.summary: Dynamic metabolic regulation for improved serine production using glycine-OFF riboswitch glyOFF6.
Enrichment of beneficial AGXT mutants over three selection cycles.
numbers_or_metrics: top three mutations reached 85, 90, and 100% of population by third cycle

events.summary: Structural analysis of AGXT26 mutations: Arg360Cys near PLP site, Asp201Gly and Asp341Gly in polypeptide binding sites, Ile20Thr affecting subunit interaction.
Synonymous mutations in AGXT26 may affect translation efficiency; higher-frequency codon introduced at 207th site.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers dynamic regulation application, directed evolution platform, mutant characterization, and structural analysis.

events.summary: Limited number of available biosensors compared to vast metabolite molecules.
Few reports of using glycine riboswitch for controlling indirectly related metabolic pathway for biosynthesis.
Developed synthetic glycine-OFF riboswitch glyOFF6 and increased-detection-range glycine-ON riboswitch glyON14 from B. subtilis library using FACS and tetA-based dual genetic selection.
High-throughput screening platform based on glyOFF6 enriched beneficial AGXT mutants; AGXT22 and AGXT26 showed 58% and 73% increased enzyme activity.
Mutations in P0/K-turn (e.g., C106U in glyON14) may influence glycine binding affinity and increase detection range.
Non-rational error-prone PCR can introduce meaningless or detrimental changes; combinatorial mutants not further combined or verified individually.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains discussion and conclusions sections summarizing key results, mutation analysis, and limitations.

events.summary: Enzyme activity assay for alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase (AGXT) was performed by measuring pyruvate formation from alanine and glyoxylate.
Fermentation conditions for 5-ALA, lactate, and serine production are specified.
Analytical methods for quantifying metabolites are described, including modified Ehrlich's reagent for 5-ALA and HPLC for other metabolites.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily contains methods details (enzyme assay, fermentation, analytical methods) and supplementary information, with no new research evolution events beyond standard protocol descriptions.
This chunk contains only reference list and publisher information, no research evolution events.

evidence_bank.quote: the existing number and performance of biosensors is far from sufficient, limiting their potential applications in metabolic engineering
we developed the synthetic glycine-ON and -OFF riboswitches for metabolic regulation and directed evolution of enzyme in Escherichia coli
only the glycine riboswitch from B. subtilis functioned as an activator to promote the downstream gfp gene expression
the tetA gene was fused with the gfp gene under the control of the B. subtilis glycine-ON riboswitch
The tetA-based dual genetic selection system was therefore successfully constructed using the B. subtilis glycine riboswitch
Glycine-ON plasmid libraries were screened with the assay of 'ON-SCREEN' and glycine-OFF plasmid libraries were screened according to the assay of 'OFF-SCREEN'
The growth performance of the strains was significantly negatively affected when the glycine concentration exceeded 107 mM
the glycine riboswitch library and backbone fragment were assembled together via Gibson assembly and transformed into E. coli MG1655 competent cell after four rounds of error-prone PCR
Glycine-ON plasmid libraries were screened with the assay of 'ON-SCREEN' and glycine-OFF plasmid libraries were screened according to the assay of 'OFF-SCREEN'
About 200 single colonies containing presumed glycine-ON riboswitches and 200 single colonies with presumed glycine-OFF riboswitches were randomly picked and cultured in 96-deep-well plates
A total of 32 mutants from the glycine-ON library exhibited higher ON/OFF values than the control
118 mutants from the glycine-OFF library exhibited significant repression compared with the wild type
glyOFF6 shown over tenfold repression compared with that of glyOFF6 in the presence of 107 mM glycine, and 54% higher than the response amplitude of the wild-type riboswitch
the detection range was increased to 107 mM from 80 mM
Glycine riboswitch mutant glyOFF6 contained five novel mutations, G42C, A62U, U79C, A92G, and U120A
glyON14, contained the four novel mutations A19G, A47C, U73A, and C106U
The concentration of lactate in W105-18A harboring pUC18-GlyON-ldhA plasmid increased more obviously with the increase of exogenous glycine
glycine riboswitch integrated into the high-copy-number plasmids had a better gradient response
The strain harboring the high-copy-number plasmid produced abundant foreign proteins, resulting in a metabolic burden for the cells and thus decreasing the cell growth

the concentrations of lactate in the strains harboring the low-copy-number plasmid P15C-GlyON-ldhA and pZY48-GlyON-ldhA were not significantly increased when the concentration of exogenous added glycine was over 7 mM
The saturation phenomenon in W105-15A and W105-48A maybe due to the low copy number of the expression plasmids, which make the glycine riboswitch insensitive to the glycine concentration
The expression of ldhA, encoding lactate dehydrogenase, was dynamically controlled under the synthetic glycine-ON riboswitch glyON14 in the high-copy-number plasmid pUC18.
Maximal lactate concentration of W105-18A and W105-15A in M9 medium supplemented with 0, 7, 13, 27, and 80 mM glycine.
lactate fermentation of W105-15A harboring P15C-GlyON-ldhA was performed under lower than 7 mM glycine.
glyOFF6 was fused with the glyA gene in pZY48 to control serine synthesis in a serine-producing strain E4G2, in which glyA gene was deleted

the expression of the glyA gene was dynamically controlled by the glycine-OFF riboswitch, realizing efficient resource utilization and an improvement of serine productivity, while also minimizing the need for chemical inducers
The expression of glyA, encoding serine hydroxymethyltransferase, was dynamically regulated under the synthetic glycine-OFF riboswitch glyOFF6
Beneficial mutations gradually accumulated after three selection cycles
The top three mutations reached about 85, 90, and 100% of the total population by the third cycle
Arg360Cys was near residue 158 in the PLP binding site
The mutation Ile20Thr of AGXT26 changed the distance between two subunits
four synonymous mutations (84th, 207th, 217th, 293rd) were also found in AGXT26
a higher-frequency codon (UCA) was introduced into the synonymous mutation at 207th site
the number of currently available biosensors is still limited in comparison with the vast number of metabolite molecules
There is few report of using a glycine riboswitch for controlling an indirectly related metabolic pathway for a biosynthesis purpose

A synthetic glycine-OFF riboswitch (glyOFF6) with significant repression in the presence of glycine and an increased-detection-range (0–107 mM) glycine-ON riboswitch (glyON14) were developed from a library based on the B. subtilis glycine riboswitch using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) and tetA-based dual genetic selection.
AGXT mutants, AGXT22 and AGXT26 with an increase of 58% and 73% enzyme activity were obtained through the synthetic high-throughput screening platform.

The mutations A4C in glyON4, A5G and A8U in glyON13, as well as C106U in glyON14 were positioned in the P0/K-turn, which might influence the glycine binding affinity and thus increase the detection range.
non-rational error-prone PCR enables us to randomly introduce many mutations, but it can also introduce meaningless or detrimental changes when multiple mutations occur
we did not further combine the mutations, nor did we verify the specific function of each mutation
The transaminase activity was determined by following the formation of pyruvate from alanine with glyoxylate as the amino acceptor.
One unit of alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase activity was defined as the amount of pyruvate (mmoL) produced per milliliter of enzyme solution extracted per $OD_{600}$ cells for one minute.
For 5-ALA shake flask fermentation, strains were incubated at 220 rpm and 37 °C in 500 mL flasks containing 50 mL of LB medium with corresponding antibiotics.
Lactate fermentation was performed in 25 mL anaerobic bottles[86].
Serine fermentation was carried out according to a previous description [68].
The 5-ALA concentration was measured using modified Ehrlich's reagent [87].

Glucose, lactate, acetate, formate, serine and pyruvate concentrations were determined by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent Technologies, USA) with RID (refractive index detector, Agilent Technologies, USA) [86].
Serine was quantified using automated precolumn derivatization with ortho-phthaldialdehyde (OPA), followed by HPLC with UV detection (Agilent Technologies, G1315D) [68].

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Lack of in vivo glycine biosensors in E. coli for dynamic metabolic regulation and directed evolution.

canonical_events.summary: Insufficient number and performance of biosensors, especially for glycine, limits metabolic engineering applications.
Synthetic glycine-ON and -OFF riboswitches can be developed from natural riboswitches for metabolic regulation and directed evolution in E. coli.
Screened four natural glycine riboswitches in E. coli with GFP reporter; only B. subtilis riboswitch functioned as an activator.
Constructed tetA-based dual genetic selection cassette using B. subtilis glycine-ON riboswitch for high-throughput screening.
key_numbers: growth rate μ decreased to 0.045 h^-1 with 90 μM Ni2+

canonical_events.summary: Generated glycine riboswitch library via four rounds of error-prone PCR and Gibson assembly.
Screened ON and OFF libraries using ON-SCREEN and OFF-SCREEN assays with FACS and dual selection; isolated top variants.
Characterized glyOFF6 (>10-fold repression, 54% higher amplitude) and glyON14 (50% higher GFP, detection range increased to 107 mM, Kd 49 mM).
Sequenced top variants; identified key mutations in structural elements (e.g., U120A may convert ON to OFF, C106U in P0/K-turn affects binding).
Applied glyON14 for tunable regulation of ldhA in lactate-producing E. coli; high-copy plasmid gave best gradient response but caused growth burden.
key_numbers: lactate 0.4 g/L at 0 mM glycine, 3.05 g/L at 80 mM

canonical_events.summary: Low-copy plasmids saturated at low glycine, limiting tunability; high-copy caused metabolic burden.
Applied glyOFF6 for dynamic regulation of glyA in serine-producing E. coli; restored growth and increased serine production by 104% (660.48 mg/L) and yield (85.40 mg/g glucose).
Developed high-throughput screening platform coupling AGXT activity to glyOFF6-controlled tetA; enriched beneficial AGXT mutants over three selection cycles.
key_numbers: top three mutations reached 85, 90, 100% by third cycle

canonical_events.summary: Structural analysis of AGXT26 mutations: Arg360Cys near PLP site, Asp201Gly and Asp341Gly in polypeptide binding sites, Ile20Thr affecting subunit interaction; synonymous mutations may affect translation.
Non-rational error-prone PCR introduced meaningless/detrimental changes; combinatorial mutants not further combined or individually verified; causal mutations not fully resolved.

edges.reason: Problem motivates hypothesis to develop glycine riboswitches.
Hypothesis leads to screening natural riboswitches.
Functional B. subtilis riboswitch enables construction of dual selection cassette.
Selection system enables library generation for directed evolution.
Library enables screening for improved variants.
Screening yields top variants for characterization.
Characterized variants are sequenced to identify mutations.
Validated glyON14 enables application in tunable lactate regulation.
Tunable regulation reveals limitations of low-copy plasmids and metabolic burden.
Validated glyOFF6 enables dynamic serine regulation.
glyOFF6 enables high-throughput screening platform for AGXT evolution.
Evolved AGXT mutants are structurally analyzed.
Mutation analysis highlights limitations of non-rational approach.
Structural analysis reveals unresolved causal mutations.

must_cover_points: Screening natural riboswitches, only B. subtilis functional
Construction of tetA dual selection system
FACS and dual selection screening to isolate glyON14 and glyOFF6
Characterization of improved dynamic range and repression
